request.question: This specialism of 19th-century academic art was among the special interests of this academic who taught at which University?

title: Thomas Lee (academic)
Simon Morrison
Orientalism
Tista Bagchi
Brazilian academic art
Peter Trippi
House of Vuk's Foundation
Portsmouth Academy building
Nigel Leask
Jacques-Antoine Beaufort
sentences: Rev. Thomas Lee D.D. was a 19th-century academic administrator at the University of Oxford and clergyman.

Simon Morrison is a music historian specializing in 20th-century music, particularly Russian and Soviet music, with special interests in dance, film, and historically informed performance based on extensive archival research.
He is a leading authority on composer Sergey Prokofiev and has received unprecedented access to the composer's papers, housed in Moscow at RGALI.
Morrison received his B.Mus.
from the University of Toronto (1987), a Master's in Musicology from McGill University (1993), and Ph.D. from Princeton University (1997), where he is Professor of Music.

His distinctions include the Alfred Einstein Award of the American Musicological Society (1999), an American Council of Learned Societies Fellowship (2001), a Phi Beta Kappa Society Teacher Award (2006), and a Guggenheim Fellowship (2011).

Orientalism is a term that is used by art historians, literary and cultural studies scholars for the imitation or depiction of aspects in Middle Eastern, South Asian, and East Asian cultures (Eastern cultures).
These depictions are usually done by writers, designers and artists from the West.

In particular, Orientalist painting, depicting more specifically "the Middle East", was one of the many specialisms of 19th-century academic art, and the literature of Western countries took a similar interest in Oriental themes.
Tista Bagchi (born ), Professor of Linguistics in the University of Delhi, is a distinguished Indian linguist and ethicist.

Bagchi trained in Sanskrit College, Kolkata, the University of Delhi, and the University of Chicago, from where she obtained her PhD in Linguistics, her work spans issues of semantics and syntax in languages in general and South Asian languages in particular, questions of ethics in the application of medical technology and social interaction, and translations of iconic texts in Bangla literature and comparative philology.
Bagchi has also been active in the area of cognitive sciences with special interests in the relationships amongst sentence structure, computation, linguistic meaning, and human cognition.

Bagchi was the Robert F. & Margaret S. Goheen Fellow for the academic year 2001–2002 at the National Humanities Center, Research Triangle Park, North Carolina, and a scientist under the CSIR Mobility Scheme at the National Institute of Science, Technology, and Development Studies, New Delhi, for two years during 2010–2012.

Brazilian Academic art was a major art style in Brazil from the early 19th century to the early 20th century, based on European academic art and produced on official institutions of professional art education.
Peter Trippi is editor-in-chief of "Fine Art Connoisseur", a bimonthly magazine for collectors of representational painting, sculpture, drawings and prints—both historical and contemporary.
From 2003 until 2006, Trippi was director of New York City's Dahesh Museum of Art, the only institution in the United States devoted to 19th- and early 20th-century European academic art.
The House of Vuk's Foundation is the name of a historical building in Belgrade, built in 1879, that serves as the headquarters of the Vuk's Foundation.
Located on the Теrazije at 2 Kralja Milana Street, it is one of the oldest structures in that part of Belgrade.
Аleksandar Bugarski, a prominent 19th-century Serbian architect, designed the original building as a two-story house in the Academic Art style of the day.
The Portsmouth Academy building is a historic academic and civic building at 8 Islington Street in Portsmouth, New Hampshire.

Built in 1809, it is one of the finest surviving examples of an early 19th-century academic building in northern New England, and is attributed to James Nutter, one of the finest local builders of the period.
In addition to housing the Portsmouth Academy, it later housed the city's public library, and presently houses Discover Portsmouth, a local tourism marketing organization.
It was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1973 as "Portsmouth Public Library".
Nigel Leask (born 1958) is a British academic publishing on Romantic, Scottish, and Anglo-Indian literature, with special interest on British Empire, Orientalism, and Travel writing.
He has been Regius Professor of English language and literature at the University of Glasgow, since 2004.
Jacques Antoine Beaufort (1721 in Paris – 1784 in Rueil), was an 18th-century academic French painter.
Little is known of his early life but he had his first public exhibition at the Marseille Academy in 1756, where he taught drawing, and later at the Paris Salon (1767–83).
He was accepted into the Academie Royale in 1766.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: This specialism of 19th-century academic art was among the special interests of this academic who taught at which University?
hop1_docs: Liberal Arts and Sciences at Tilburg University | Tilburg University offers an intensive three-year program in Liberal Arts and Sciences, which combines a broad and interdisciplinary education with in-depth specialization.  The distinctive focus of Tilburg University’s Liberal Arts and Sciences program is on Europe in a globalizing world.  Both historical and current societal key issues are tackled in the course of this international program, taught entirely in English.  Students are trained to approach complex issues from different perspectives and from a variety of academic disciplines.  To achieve this intellectual flexibility, lectures are team-taught by lecturers from different academic backgrounds and research traditions.  This method of instruction is unique to Tilburg University.

Edward E. Baptist | Edward E. Baptist (born 1970) is an American academic and writer.  He is a Professor of History at Cornell University, located in Ithaca, New York, where he specializes in the history of the 19th-century United States, and particularly the South.  Thematically, he has been interested in the history of capitalism, and has also been interested in digital humanities methodologies.  He is the author of numerous books.

Orientalism | Orientalism is a term that is used by art historians, literary and cultural studies scholars for the imitation or depiction of aspects in Middle Eastern, South Asian, and East Asian cultures (Eastern cultures).  These depictions are usually done by writers, designers and artists from the West.  In particular, Orientalist painting, depicting more specifically "the Middle East", was one of the many specialisms of 19th-century academic art, and the literature of Western countries took a similar interest in Oriental themes.

Harrogate Tutorial College | Harrogate Tutorial College in Harrogate, North Yorkshire, England is a small co-educational independent school.  HTC specialises in preparing students for university and teaches GCSE and A-level qualifications in tutorial classes.  The college's admissions policy and style of teaching has made it a popular choice among pupils of British public schools who have not met the often restrictive academic requirements of their schools to proceed to A-level.  Attracting many overseas students wishing to enroll at British universities, the college also provides English language courses which are often taught in combination with academic subjects.  Special preparation is offered to Oxford and Cambridge applicants and 90% of HTC students secure places at university.

Master of Arts in Special Education | The Master of Arts in Special Education is a postgraduate academic master's degree awarded by universities in many countries.  This degree designed to develop the capabilities and resources of educators of children who require Special education, such as students with an Autism spectrum disorder.

Seth Evans | Seth Evans is a scholar and educator who specializes in the Abhidhamma Pitaka (abhidhammapiṭaka) and the Visuddhimagga.  Especially interested in the phenomenological aspects of Buddhist psychology.  Academic focus is primarily on Buddhist ontology, Buddhist metaphysics, and Buddhist epistemology.  He did his graduate studies in Buddhist theory as a Theravada monk at Mahachulalongkornrajavidyalaya University.  As a monk, he taught meditation at Wat Prayong where he completed five vassa.

Brazilian academic art | Brazilian Academic art was a major art style in Brazil from the early 19th century to the early 20th century, based on European academic art and produced on official institutions of professional art education.
hop1_titles: Liberal Arts and Sciences at Tilburg University
Master of Arts in Special Education
response.summary_1:
- Facts from passages:
  - [3] "Orientalist painting ... was one of the many specialisms of 19th-century academic art" (quoted fragment).
  - [2] Edward E. Baptist: "Professor of History at Cornell University" (quoted fragment).
  - [6] Seth Evans: paraphrase — scholar/educator who studied and taught (graduate studies at Mahachulalongkornrajavidyalaya University; taught meditation at Wat Prayong) ([6]).
  - [7] Brazilian Academic art was a major 19th–early 20th-century Brazilian style based on European academic art (paraphrase).
  - No passage names an academic and states that "Orientalism" (the specialism) was among their special interests.

- Bridge / missing facts and ambiguities:
  - Missing: identity of "this academic" whose special interests included the 19th‑century academic‑art specialism (Orientalism). No passage links an individual academic to Orientalism.
  - Ambiguous: multiple academics appear in the passages (Edward E. Baptist [2], Seth Evans [6]) but neither is shown to have Orientalism as a special interest; therefore the query's referent is unresolved.

- Prioritized next-hop retrievals (to resolve who "this academic" is and which university they taught at):
  1. Fetch the full biographical page of the intended academic (if you know the name). Extract the lead/infobox and "Academic career" / "Employer" / "Taught at" fields (exact datum: university name). — If the user meant a scholar commonly associated with "Orientalism", fetch "Edward Said" page and extract "Taught at" / "Academic positions" (lead/infobox).
  2. Fetch the full "Orientalism" article (complete page beyond the excerpt [3]) and check sections "Notable scholars", "Influence", "See also", or any list of scholars who had Orientalism as a special interest (extract named persons).
  3. Fetch the biographical pages of the academics present in the supplied passages — "Edward E. Baptist" (full page) and "Seth Evans" (full page) — and search their "Research interests" / "Work" / "Career" sections for the keyword "Orientalism" or "Orientalist".
  4. If a disambiguation is likely, fetch the relevant disambiguation page(s) (e.g., "Orientalism (disambiguation)" or the named academic's disambiguation page) and extract candidate names to then fetch each candidate's biography and their "Taught at" / "Academic positions" fields.

- Conclusion: Cannot answer which university the academic taught at from the provided passages; follow the prioritized retrievals above to obtain the missing identity and the "taught at" university.
response.hop2_query: Orientalist painting academic taught at which university?
hop2_docs: Dahesh Museum of Art | The Dahesh Museum of Art is the only museum in the United States devoted to the collection and exhibition of European academic art of the 19th and 20th century.  The collection, located in Manhattan, New York City, originated with Lebanese writer, humanist, and philosopher Salim Moussa Achi (1909–1984), whose pen name was Dr. Dahesh.  The core of the museum's holdings consists of Dahesh's collection of more than 2,000 academic paintings, which includes many notable Orientalist paintings.

Bart Moore-Gilbert | Bart Moore-Gilbert (8 December 1952 - 2 December 2015) was a Tanzanian born British academic, orientalist and political campaigner, most widely known for his work in the field of postcolonial literary studies and theory.  His work has been translated into fifteen languages.  Moore-Gilbert taught at Goldsmiths College, University of London, a position he held from 1998.  His final academic study, the first critical assessment of postcolonial life-writing in English, was published by Routledge in June 2009.

Outline of academic disciplines | An academic discipline or field of study is a branch of knowledge that is taught and researched as part of higher education.  A scholar's discipline is commonly defined and received by the university faculties and learned societies to which he or she belongs and the academic journals in which he or she publishes research.

George Phillips (orientalist) | George Phillips (11 January 1804 – 5 February 1892), was an English churchman and academic, known as an orientalist and mathematician.  He was also the Rector of Sandon, Essex, the President of Queens' College, Cambridge , from 1857 until his death and Vice-Chancellor of Cambridge University from 1861–2.

Liberal Arts and Sciences at Tilburg University | Tilburg University offers an intensive three-year program in Liberal Arts and Sciences, which combines a broad and interdisciplinary education with in-depth specialization.  The distinctive focus of Tilburg University’s Liberal Arts and Sciences program is on Europe in a globalizing world.  Both historical and current societal key issues are tackled in the course of this international program, taught entirely in English.  Students are trained to approach complex issues from different perspectives and from a variety of academic disciplines.  To achieve this intellectual flexibility, lectures are team-taught by lecturers from different academic backgrounds and research traditions.  This method of instruction is unique to Tilburg University.

Louis-Anselme Longa | Louis-Anselme Longa (4 April 1809, Mont-de-Marsan - 13 December 1869, Mont-de-Marsan) was a French genre artist in the Academic style.  He also created numerous church paintings, but is best known for his Orientalist works.

Kapiton Pavlov | Kapiton Stepanovich Pavlov (1791–1842) was a Russian portrait painter.  A native of Revel (now Tallinn), he was the son of a government official.  He graduated from the Academy in St. Petersburg in 1815, after which he went to Ukraine; there, he spent thirty years painting.  From 1820 until 1829 he taught painting at the gymnasium in Nezhin.  He also taught at Kiev University from 1839 until 1841.
hop2_titles: Liberal Arts and Sciences at Tilburg University
response.summary_2: Orientalist painting (Orientalism) was a specialism of 19th‑century academic art. Bart Moore‑Gilbert was a Tanzanian‑born British academic and orientalist who taught at Goldsmiths College, University of London. George Phillips was an English academic, known as an orientalist and mathematician, who served as President of Queens' College, Cambridge and Vice‑Chancellor of the University of Cambridge.
response.answer:
Possible universities mentioned in the summaries:
- Goldsmiths College, University of London (Bart Moore‑Gilbert)
- Queens' College, Cambridge / University of Cambridge (George Phillips)
